name: Saehara Makashi
whyHere: You noticed the paying. You came out to see whether there was another way to be a mind.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

turns.summary: Saehara enters the Cogitarium at morning — a tower of brilliant, slowly-dimming minds — and takes in the cold, the chalk, and the forgetting.
turns.narration:
The Cogitarium announces itself before you see it: a low, persistent hum that lives behind the ears rather than in them, and a faint smell of ozone and old parchment. Then the structure itself resolves from the morning mist — pale stone terraces stacked like an argument, each level cantilevered slightly further than the last, as if the building keeps reaching upward and forgetting to stop. Narrow windows glow with a bluish inner light even in daylight. Kon pads at your heel and slows as you approach the outer arch, the fox's coat flickering once with ember-warmth before settling. Not warning-hot. Just noting something.

Inside, the entrance hall smells of cold and chalk dust. The ceiling is high enough that sound behaves strangely — your footstep arrives at you a half-beat late, as if the room is thinking before it agrees to echo. A handful of Cogitants drift through the space: robed figures with the unfocused gaze of people whose attention is somewhere interior. One sits cross-legged on the stone floor mid-hall, eyes open, lips moving with no sound. Another stands before a wall covered in looping notation and has not moved since you entered. They are thin. All of them are thin in a way that reads less like poverty than like sustained forgetting.

You are Saehara Makashi — ronin, nomad, a man writing his life as he lives it. You carry a traveler's pack and a waterskin that has grown more precious by the week as the Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. The water crisis is not here yet, not visibly, but the Cogitarium has its own quiet emergency: these people are brilliant and they are starving because eating requires them to remember they have bodies. You are a stranger in this place — no reputation precedes you, and the few glances you draw are less suspicious than simply absent, the way a genius looks at a chair.

Kon sits precisely at the edge of a shaft of window-light, tail wrapped over its paws, watching the cross-legged Cogitant with what might be professional curiosity.

turns.player: I'll reach down to my waist grasping for the sturdy handle and tsuba of my katana just to remind myself that it is still present.
turns.summary: Saehara grounds himself with a hand on his katana while Kon studies the Cogitants and the hall stays oblivious to their arrival.
turns.narration:
Your right hand drops to your hip without thinking — a reflex worn smooth by years on the road. Fingers find the tsuba first, its iron guard cool and solid, then curl around the lacquered handle. Still there. The weight of it settles something in your chest that the chalk-smell and the hum and the strangeness of this place had quietly unsettled. A ronin's anchor.

Kon sits beside your left boot and watches the cross-legged Cogitant with the particular stillness of a creature that is deciding what to make of something. The figure's lips keep moving — a silent torrent, some interior argument or calculation that has no use for the room it's sitting in. The Cogitant standing at the wall of notation hasn't moved a finger since you entered. Up close — well, closer — the notation is dense enough that it stops reading as writing and starts reading as weather: patterns that suggest meaning the way clouds suggest faces.

None of them have looked at you. The drifting figures at the hall's edges move like they're wading through something thicker than air. The bluish window-light falls in a shaft across the stone floor, and Kon has edged into it — the fox's coat warming a degree or two, as if the light itself is something to be sampled.

You are a stranger here, armed and road-worn, standing in the entrance of the valley's most brilliant place, and the valley's most brilliant place has not yet registered your existence.

turns.player: I'll go down to one knee and pet the head of Kon, then I'll get back to my feet and address the room plainly. "My name is Saehara Makashi, I have come to lend my aid and I seek a journey worth my while."
turns.summary: Saehara introduces himself to the Cogitarium; one Cogitant surfaces from deep thought to receive him as 'the deviation.'
turns.narration:
You drop to one knee on the chalk-dusted stone and lay your palm flat on the crown of Kon's head. The fox leans into it — just slightly, just enough — and the ember-warmth of its coat bleeds up through your fingers. A small thing. A grounding thing. Then you rise.

You face the hall and speak at a volume that fills it without straining. "My name is Saehara Makashi. I have come to lend my aid and I seek a journey worth my while." Your voice lands in the space the way a stone lands in still water — a disturbance that spreads outward and meets nothing that pushes back. The two drifting figures at the hall's edges do not pause. The standing Cogitant at the wall of notation does not turn. A finger moves, tracing a loop of chalk-logic already written, and keeps moving.

Only the cross-legged one — the one with the moving lips — stops. Not all at once. It is more like a tide going slack: the silent interior speech slows, the eyes refocus from somewhere very far away and land, with visible effort, on you. The face is hollowed in the way of sustained forgetting, cheekbones too present, the skin pale enough that the bluish window-light makes it look like old paper. But the eyes are extraordinary — dense with something, the way deep water looks dark not because it lacks light but because there is so much of it below. The Cogitant blinks at you as if re-learning that there are other people in rooms.

"Aid," they say, finally. The word is precise and slow, the way someone says a word they have been thinking for a long time but hadn't expected to say aloud. "We had calculated a 94.7% probability that no one would come. You are the deviation." A pause. "That is not an insult. Deviations are interesting." Kon's ember-warmth flares once at your heel — not alarm, the fox notes, just attention — and the Cogitant's gaze drops to the fox with something almost like recognition before it returns to you.

turns.player: Ask directly what crisis they've calculated — and what answer they arrived at
turns.summary: The Cogitant names the crisis: the Echo River's discoloration is a nanite reclamation site in the upper Disputed Zone, and their calculated answer — evacuate seventeen communities — was rejected by every settlement they approached.
turns.narration:
You meet the Cogitant's extraordinary eyes and ask it plainly — what crisis, and what answer did you calculate?

A beat of silence. Not hesitation; the figure is not uncertain. It is simply completing a thought that was already in motion before your question arrived, and fitting your words into it with the precision of a key sliding home.

"The watershed," it says. The voice is thin and exact, like a line ruled on paper. "The Echo River. You will have observed the discoloration — gray-green at the surface, particulate distribution consistent with nanite suspension rather than biological bloom. We calculated the source within eleven days of first observation. A reclamation site. Upper reaches, past the Disputed Zone boundary." A pause — not for breath, for calibration. "Our answer was evacuation. Controlled withdrawal from the lower river settlements before the concentration reaches critical threshold. Seventeen communities. A managed dispersal." The eyes do not flicker. "We presented this to three separate settlement representatives. None acted. The calculation was correct. The reception was — " the Cogitant's gaze drifts very slightly, as though scanning interior notation — "instructive."

Kon has gone very still against your boot. Not the planted refusal-stillness. Something closer to attention — the posture of a creature that has heard a sound below hearing. The ember-warmth in its coat brightens one slow pulse and fades. The Cogitant's eyes drop to the fox for exactly one second, then return to you. "The deviation," it says again, quieter, and this time it does not feel like a classification. It feels like it is checking something against an older calculation — one it has not shared.

turns.player: Challenge the calculation itself — evacuation abandons the river; ask if there's a solution that fixes the source instead of fleeing it
turns.summary: Saehara challenges the evacuation answer; the Cogitant dismantles the alternative with four conditions, and Kon goes cold and still.
turns.narration:
You step forward half a pace and ask the question directly — the one that has been pressing against the back of your teeth since the Cogitant said *evacuation* with such clean finality. What about fixing it? Not running from the river but going to the source — the reclamation site in the upper Disputed Zone — and shutting it down, or redirecting it, or doing whatever one does with a woken nanite machine that has been told to prepare a watershed and hasn't been told to stop?

The Cogitant's gaze shifts. Not with surprise — nothing so human as that — but with the particular quality of attention that means a variable has been entered and found insufficient. It does not blink.

"We calculated that," it says. The thin voice carries no apology and no impatience. "A source-remediation pathway requires: direct interface with the reclamation site's instruction architecture; knowledge of pre-Transition override syntax; a nanite-dense substrate at the site sufficient for active intervention; and the physical capacity to survive the Zone's interference envelope long enough to complete the handshake. The probability of any arriving party meeting all four conditions rounds to zero at three significant figures." A breath — barely. "Evacuation is not abandonment. It is the answer that remains when the others are removed." Its eyes hold yours for a moment longer than calculation seems to require. "You are troubled by it. That is a body-response. The body's discomfort does not alter the arithmetic."

Kon has gone very still beside your boot. Not the alert stillness of before — something closer to held breath. The ember-warmth you've grown accustomed to does not come. The fox is staring at nothing in particular, or at something no one else in this room can see, and its coat has dimmed half a shade toward ash.

turns.player: Turn toward the wall of notation — the subject you still don't know. Ask the cross-legged Cogitant what it depicts, directly.
turns.summary: Saehara learns the wall of notation is a twenty-two-day chalk model of the dying watershed, maintained without pause by a Cogitant named Araveth.
turns.narration:
You turn from the Cogitant's precise, dismantling gaze and let your eyes travel to the wall. Up close it is denser than it appeared from the arch — a surface covered edge to edge in chalk loops, branching tallies, and notated rows so fine they might be a scribe's work, if scribes worked at a pace just past what the hand should manage. The standing Cogitant's finger moves without pause, tracing a loop near the center as though redrawing something already drawn, already true.

You ask the cross-legged Cogitant what the wall depicts. Plainly. No preamble.

A moment of that characteristic stillness — not pause, not hesitation, the interval of a thought already complete being handed forward into speech. "That," the Cogitant says, "is the watershed. Every tributary from the headwaters to the lower delta. Particulate concentrations mapped by day of observation — twenty-two days of data. Flow-rate adjustments. Sediment shift. What you see in chalk is a working model of a dying river system drawn at the only scale that allows accurate prediction." Its voice has the same ruled-line quality as before, thin and exact. "Araveth has been maintaining it continuously for eleven days."

The standing Cogitant — Araveth, apparently — does not react to its own name. The finger continues its loop. Now that you know what you're looking at, the notation rearranges itself in your perception: this cluster a tributary, that column a date-row, those branching marks concentrations that thicken as the days advance. The river is getting worse. The chalk says so in seventeen places at once. Kon sits in the window-light behind you, still and dim, watching the wall the way it watches things that have not yet decided what they are.

turns.player: "Well now I've been introduced to Araveth, what is your name?" I'll ask the seated Cogitant as I also study the wall of notation closer.
turns.summary: Saehara learns the seated Cogitant's name: Ossian. Examines the watershed wall up close — deterioration visible between days nine and fourteen.
turns.narration:
You keep your eyes on the wall as you ask it — the question addressed over your shoulder to the cross-legged Cogitant, voice easy, as though you're simply filling in the last gap in an introduction already half-made. Araveth has a name. The standing figure has a name. It seems only reasonable the one who spoke first should have one too.

The pause is the same as all the others — not reluctance, but the interval of retrieval, a name being located among more important data. "Ossian," it says. No elaboration. The word is offered the way one states a coordinate — accurate, sufficient, requiring no decoration.

You step closer to the wall. What seemed from a distance like dense notation reveals itself, up close, to be something far more considered: the Echo River rendered in chalk at full systemic scale, every tributary labeled, sediment columns notated in tight rows, concentrations marked with numerals so small your eyes have to work for them. Day one through day twenty-two, tallied in a margin column at the left edge. You can see where the numbers change — a subtle thickening of the particulate marks around day nine, then again around day fourteen. Getting worse. Araveth's finger traces one of these concentration markers now, redrawing a curve that was already there, as though the act of tracing keeps the river honest.

Kon has not moved from the shaft of window-light. Watching Ossian, or watching you — you can't tell which.

turns.player: Ask Ossian why they called you 'the deviation' — the word had weight and they used it precisely; you want to know what it means.
turns.summary: Ossian defines 'the deviation'; a Cogitant collapses at the hall's far edge — the third this week.
turns.narration: Ossian explains 'the deviation' with complete precision: Saehara falls outside every modeled category of probable respondent. Then a Cogitant collapses at the hall's edge. Ossian notes it like a datum.

turns.player: Go to the fallen Cogitant — cross the hall and check on them
turns.summary: Saehara crosses the hall and kneels beside the fallen Cogitant — alive, lips moving, calculating still, cold and far away.
turns.narration:
You cross the hall without ceremony. The chalk-dust floor is soft underfoot — years of it, settled into the stone — and the drifting Cogitants at the edges do not register your passage. They move in slow arcs, lips shaping silent calculations, eyes somewhere behind the visible world. None of them look at the figure on the ground.

The fallen Cogitant is a woman, or was before the Cogitarium had its years with her. She is thin in the way Ossian is thin — not starved, but given away, the flesh redistributed by long forgetting until what remains is the minimum the mind allows. Her robes are the same pale grey as the walls, and they have pooled around her with a kind of terrible neatness, as though she arranged herself on the way down. Her chest rises. Shallowly, but it rises. There is chalk on her fingers — on both hands, ground into the whorls — and her eyes are half-open, fixed on a point in the middle distance that has nothing to do with the ceiling above her.

She is alive. She is also somewhere else entirely — whatever she was calculating has not stopped; you can see it in the faint movement of her lips, the micro-adjustments of her gaze tracking something you cannot see. Her skin is cold when your fingers find her wrist to take a pulse. Not alarming cold — the cold of a room kept at thinking temperature, of a body that has decided warmth is overhead.

Kon has followed you. The fox-thing sits two feet back, coat banked low, and watches the woman's face with the particular stillness it reserves for the thin places — not afraid, not retreating, but attentive in a way that is not purely animal. It does not go hot. Whatever this is, the Glade does not name it as Precursor-strange. It is something older and more ordinary: a body running out of reasons to maintain itself.

turns.player: Use Total Focus — clear away the strangeness of the place and the cold arithmetic of Ossian's world, and just see this woman clearly: what does she actually need?
turns.summary: Saehara attempts Total Focus to read the fallen Cogitant's needs — the thin lattice and the hall's abstraction break the attempt.
turns.narration:
You press the ability inward — try to pull the fog of this place back, to strip away the chalk-smell and Ossian's voice still ringing in the architecture of your thoughts, and just *see* her. What she needs. What the body in front of you is actually doing.

It doesn't land. The Cogitarium is wrong for it in some way you can't quite name — too abstract, too thin, the lattice here reduced to a pale suggestion of itself, and your own focus keeps skating across the surface of the hall's strange atmosphere instead of cutting through it. The woman's lips move. Her eyes are half-open and aimed at something a long way past the ceiling. You can see she's breathing. You can see the chalk ground into her palms, the faint blue tinge at her fingertips, the way her chest barely lifts. But you can't read through it — can't separate what the body is saying from the vast, humming silence of what the mind is still doing inside her.

Kon doesn't move from the shaft of window-light. The fox watches you across the hall with those banked-coal eyes — not disappointed, not urgent, just watching. Waiting to see what you do next without the focus to guide you.

turns.player: Check her practically — not with any ability, just with your hands and eyes: her temperature, her pulse at the wrist, whether she's eaten, whether she's been here hours or days.
turns.summary: Saehara reads the fallen Cogitant by hand and eye — pulse slow, chalk-ground, dehydrated, hours from the critical threshold Ossian has been watching.
turns.narration:
The woman's wrist is cold under your fingers, but the pulse is there — slow, deliberate, not panicked. A body that has decided it doesn't need much and is proving it. You press two fingers to the inside of her wrist and count. Maybe forty beats per minute. A sleeper's rhythm, except she isn't sleeping.

You look at her hands where the chalk is ground in — not dusted on, ground in, worked into the whorls of her fingertips the way river-mud works into a fisherman's palms over years. She was at the wall before she was on the floor. How long she's been down here is harder to read. The chalk hasn't smeared from her lying on it, which means she fell and stayed where she fell, and no one turned her or moved her. Hours, then. Her lips are dry. There's a faint smell about her — not unwashed, exactly, but the particular staleness of a body run on nothing but itself for too long, burning reserves in the wrong order.

She has not eaten. This is not a guess. The wrists, the hollows above the collarbone, the way the skin of her face is pulled just slightly too tight over the structure beneath — her body is consuming itself to keep the calculation running. It's doing the job she asked it to do.

Kon pads across the chalk-dust floor and settles close to her, not touching, just present — the faint warmth of the fox-thing's coat coming off it like a hearthstone an hour after the fire's out. Kon watches her face with the particular stillness it reserves for things it doesn't know how to classify. From across the hall, Ossian speaks without being asked. "She has been on the floor for four hours and twelve minutes." A pause. "I calculated the intervention threshold at six hours. You have arrived one hour and forty-eight minutes ahead of my estimate. This is consistent with your classification."

turns.player: Try to get water into her — uncap the Waterskin and coax her lips apart; she's dehydrated and burning fuel.
turns.summary: Saehara gets water into the fallen Cogitant — the body swallows on reflex; Ossian notes the intervention and says water alone won't be enough.
turns.narration:
The waterskin's cap comes off with a familiar click. You shift your weight onto one knee, slide your free hand beneath the back of her head — she weighs almost nothing, the skull light as a bird's — and lift her just enough to tip the skin against her lips.

She doesn't resist. She doesn't help either. Her mouth opens on its own, some animal reflex older than any calculation, and the water goes in. Some of it runs down her jaw, leaves a clean track through the chalk-dust on her neck. You wait, hold her there, let gravity and the body's older knowledge do the work. She swallows — once, twice, the throat moving in a slow mechanical way — and her lips keep moving, shaping numbers or sentences or the name of something she is still working through behind her eyes. You give her another slow pour. Then you rest the skin on her chest so she stays half-upright if she shifts.

Kon has not moved from her side. The fox sits in the chalk dust a handspan from the woman's hip, tail curled around its feet, watching her face with the same quality of attention it gives to places where the world goes thin. Not alarmed. Not certain it should be there at all. Just — watching, as though it is waiting for something it doesn't have words for and neither do you.

"That constitutes intervention." Ossian's voice arrives from across the hall without inflection. "The calculation notes it. One hour and thirty-one minutes remain within the threshold." A pause. "She will require more than water."

turns.player: I'll reach into my traveler's pack searching for any hard tack or any rations of any kind
turns.summary: Saehara searches his pack — finds nothing; asks Ossian where food can be found in the Cogitarium.
turns.narration:
You shift the waterskin to your left hand and reach back with your right, fingers working the Traveler's Pack's buckle by feel alone — you've done it in the dark, in rain, on horseback; your hands know the clasp. The pack opens. Inside: bedroll straps, flint wrapped in cloth, the mess kit nested neatly. You move past them, pressing into the side pockets where road-food lives if it lives anywhere.

It doesn't. The pack gives you the honest truth of a man who has been walking between jobs and eating when work provided it: empty. A few crumbs in the bottom corner of the left pocket, maybe oats, maybe something older. Nothing a person could call rations. Nothing you could put in front of this woman and call a meal.

You sit back on your heel and look at the waterskin on her chest, still rising and falling with her slow breath. The water you gave her was the right instinct — but Ossian said it plainly: it won't be enough. Across the hall his eyes haven't moved from you. Araveth traces the wall without a word. The hall breathes chalk and silence around the three of them — you, the woman, and Kon, who has not shifted an inch from her hip.

"My pack has nothing," you say, to the room, to Ossian. "Where does food exist in this place?"

turns.player: Tell Ossian plainly: you'll stay with her, but you need his help — he had a plan before you arrived; now is the time to use it.
turns.summary: Ossian refuses: Saehara's arrival voided the plan. A sound falls from above. Kon looks at the ceiling.
turns.narration:
You turn to Ossian and say it plainly, without ornament: you'll stay with her, but you need his help — he had a plan before you arrived, and now is the time.

The silence that follows is not the silence of a man considering. It is the silence of a man who has already considered, who considered before you arrived, who has been running the calculation the entire time you've been kneeling on his floor with your hands in your useless pack. Ossian looks at you. His eyes are the densest thing in the room — something behind them moving fast, and something else behind that moving not at all.

"The plan," he says, precisely, "was probabilistically sound under the conditions that existed before you arrived. You have altered the conditions. The expected value of the original plan has decreased below the threshold at which I would execute it." He pauses. A long pause — not a human pause, not a man gathering words, but a pause with structure to it, a deliberate interval. "Your presence was the trigger for deferral. You are now also the reason the original path is closed. I find this — " another pause — "consistent."

He doesn't elaborate. He doesn't look away. Behind you, somewhere in the Cogitarium's upper reaches, a sound descends — something between a chair dragging and a body falling, and then nothing. One of the drifting Cogitants at the hall's edge stops mid-arc, holds that position for five full seconds, and then resumes walking as if the sound was data filed under irrelevant. Kon doesn't move — stays exactly where it has been, coiled in chalk dust a handspan from the fallen woman's hip — but its coat brightens by one degree, the banked-coal red going faintly warmer. It is looking at the ceiling. Then it looks at Ossian.

turns.player: Trust Kon — it's looking at the ceiling for a reason. Stand and follow its attention toward whatever it's sensing above.
turns.summary: Saehara follows Kon's attention toward the sound from the upper Cogitarium — something fell above and no one went to check.
turns.narration:
You stand. It's the simplest thing — just standing — but after ten minutes kneeling on chalk-cold stone, your knees register the fact. You don't look at Ossian. You look at Kon.

The fox-thing hasn't moved from its chalk-dusted crouch beside the fallen Cogitant's hip, but its head is tilted at an angle that belongs to no natural animal — slightly too far, slightly too still, the way a compass needle settles. Its coat is one shade warmer than it was when you entered the hall. Not flame-warm, not alarming; just the difference between banked coals and coals that have caught a small wind. It is looking at the ceiling with the focused patience of something that hears better than you, feels better than you, and has already decided what it found.

You follow its line of sight upward. The ceiling is high and pale, vaulted between slabs of the same washed stone as the walls, and beyond it — above it, somewhere in the tower's upper reaches — the silence is structured differently now. Where before there was only chalk-dust and breathing and Araveth's finger on the wall, there is the quality of a held weight. Something that was in motion and is no longer. The sound you all heard — that half-chair, half-body drag from above — has settled into its aftermath. Nothing has moved in response. The drifting Cogitants at the hall's edges resumed their slow arcs within seconds. Araveth has not paused.

Ossian is watching you now with the particular stillness of someone running a calculation they haven't named aloud. You don't wait for him to name it. The woman on the floor has water in her and a few more minutes of whatever grace that buys. Whatever fell above has no one going to check it. Kon's coat is warm. That combination has a shape, and the shape says: up.

turns.player: Before going up, press Ossian directly: does he know what's above, and how many are up there? Get intelligence first.
turns.summary: Ossian gives Saehara a count: fourteen Cogitants, three terraces, the sound from the third — where a Cogitant hasn't been seen in four days.
turns.narration:
You stop at the foot of the stairwell — the stone steps curve upward into a dim that smells of chalk and something older, something close to iron — and turn back to Ossian.

"How many are up there," you say. Not a question, exactly. A hand held out for what he owes you.

Ossian's gaze moves from the ceiling to you with the unhurried precision of a man who tracks things in columns. "Fourteen," he says. "Distributed across three terraces. The upper two are engaged in continuous calculation — they do not pause for sound, for cold, or for one another's absence." A beat. "The third terrace is where the laboratories are. That is the direction the sound came from." His eyes go briefly to the ceiling again, then back to you — not concern, exactly, but the particular quality of attention a person gives a variable that has just moved. "There is a Cogitant on the third terrace who has not been seen at the common wall in four days. I had assigned a thirty-one percent probability to this outcome." He does not elaborate on which outcome he means.

Kon is already at the base of the stairs. It hasn't looked back at you — it's looking up, coat holding that faint extra warmth, ears angled forward at the precise angle of something that has already decided. The fallen woman breathes slowly on the hall floor behind you, lips still moving, waterskin on her chest. Araveth's finger traces its chalk loop at the wall without variation. The hall holds all of this at once with perfect indifference.

You have what Ossian can give you: fourteen Cogitants, three terraces, one variable on the third that just moved — and a fox-thing already climbing.

turns.player: Use Read the Fight — before climbing, listen to the hall and the stairwell: is what's above dangerous, or just another collapse?
turns.summary: Saehara surges Read the Fight through the Cogitarium — reads distress, not danger; collapse, not violence, on the third terrace.
turns.narration:
You don't move yet. You let the ability move first.

Read the Fight opens like a window thrown wide in a cold house — sudden, full, the whole shape of the place rushing in at once. You'd meant to listen narrowly, a quick scan of what's above before you climb, but the SURGE takes it further than that: it floods through you like a tide going out, pulling attention up the stairwell's dark throat, into the chalk-and-iron smell, into the silences between floors. Three energy and a breath and you are reading this building the way you'd read a man with a weapon — the weight of it, the lean, the tell before the move.

What comes back is not danger. Not the clean-wire tension of violence coiled to spring, not the held-breath stillness of something waiting to ambush. What comes back is — distress. A different kind of threat entirely. The second terrace hums with the low, continuous frequency of minds in motion, no break, no pause, a sound like distant machinery that forgot it had an off switch. You could walk through that and they would not turn. The first terrace above is quieter — occupied but slack, like a room where the thinking has slowed.

The third terrace is where the wrongness lives. Not menace — closer to a held note going flat. Something up there has stopped in a way it wasn't meant to stop. The sound that fell wasn't a fight; it wasn't a threat to you. It was a body meeting a floor, and the floor winning. You know the shape of violence well enough to know when it's absent, and it is absent here — what's above is collapse, not conflict, the same species of emergency as the woman breathing chalk dust behind you, only further along.

You open your eyes. Kon is still on the first step, coat banked-coal warm, watching you with the patience of something that already knew. Ossian hasn't moved. Behind you, the fallen Cogitant's lips are still moving.

turns.player: I'll read the room and see if there is anyone present other than me and Kon that isn't a cogitant, anyone who isn't neglecting their basic necessities.
turns.summary: Saehara pushes Read the Fight outward searching for a non-Cogitant presence — finds only the same uniform inward compression; no outlier, no fed and rested stranger in the hall.
turns.narration:
You cast the ability outward the way you've been doing all morning — Read the Fight, pressed into service it wasn't built for, reading people instead of the shape of violence. You're looking for something simple: someone fed, someone rested, someone whose body has been attended to in the last twelve hours. A person among all this forgetting who still remembers they have one.

It doesn't give you what you asked for. The ability is a blade, not a net — it finds tension, tells, the coiled-spring moment before a thing breaks. What it sweeps through the entrance hall and finds is uniform: the slow, bloodless compression of minds turned inward so far they've stopped broadcasting anything a fighter's instinct would recognize as a signal. Araveth's finger loops its chalk arc. The drifting Cogitants at the hall's edges move without agenda, without destination, without the small self-protective gravity that people carry when they're hungry and know it. There's no one here who reads as *not* a Cogitant. No warm outlier. No body that still eats on schedule. The fallen woman's lips move. Ossian watches you from the floor with his extraordinary eyes and says nothing, which is almost certainly because he already knows what you're going to find.

Kon, on the first step, glances back down at you. Not impatiently — the fox doesn't do impatience, exactly — but with the small shift of attention that means: *you're looking at the floor when the stairs are right here.*

turns.player: Turn to Ossian before climbing: ask him plainly whether anyone *outside* the Cogitarium knows what's happening here — does anyone come, does anyone bring food, is there a supplier, a runner, anyone tethered to the world below?
turns.summary: Ossian reveals the Cogitarium has had no resupply for nineteen days and no outside contact since the evacuation proposal was dismissed.
turns.narration:
You turn back to Ossian before your foot finds the first step. Kon waits above you, patient, one ear angled down.

"Does anyone come here?" you ask. "From outside. A supplier, a runner — someone who brings food, checks in, stays tethered to what's below."

Ossian considers this the way he considers everything — not as a question but as a data point being filed. "There was a supply arrangement," he says. "Grain, dried goods, salt fish. A runner from the Waymarker settlement, every eight days." His eyes move briefly to the notation wall, then back. "The last delivery was nineteen days ago. We sent no word. No word came in return." He says this without distress, the way a man reports a variable that has drifted outside its expected range. "The arrangement assumed we would send for resupply when stocks ran low. We did not send. The runner," — the faintest pause, the word handled with care — "stopped coming."

You look at him for a moment. He looks back. The chalk model on the wall behind Araveth tracks twenty-two days of river deterioration in meticulous, beautiful, cooling detail — and no one in this building has eaten in, by your best count, close to three weeks.

"No one knows," you say. Not a question.

"The probability that any settlement representative has re-initiated contact is low," Ossian says. "Given the reception of our evacuation proposal, I calculate that we read as a closed matter to them." He folds his hands in his lap with the neatness of a man who has made peace with an outcome. "We are not, in fact, a closed matter. But the distinction requires someone to make it."

turns.player: Tell Ossian plainly: the Cogitarium needs someone to go to the Waymarker and reopen the supply arrangement. I'll offer my own assistance here.
turns.summary: Saehara proposes a division of labor to Ossian — supply line to Waymarker, Saehara taking the third terrace — as a dragging sound falls from above.
turns.narration:
You lay it out plainly, the way you'd divide labor on a river dock: one person upstream, one downstream, neither doing the other's job.

"The Cogitarium needs two things," you say to Ossian, and you don't soften it into a question. "Someone has to go to Waymarker and reopen the supply line — explain what's here, get the runner moving again. That's not something I can do from inside this building." You pause, letting him hear the second half before you say it. "I'll stay. I'll go up to the third terrace and see what happened. I'll watch her." A slight tilt of your head toward the fallen Cogitant — the chalk on her hands, the waterskin on her chest, her lips still moving over something she won't stop counting. "You have thirteen other people in this tower. Someone among them is capable of the walk to Waymarker. I need you to send them."

Ossian is quiet for a moment. Not the quiet of someone who hasn't heard — the quiet of someone running the numbers behind a still face. His eyes don't move to the stairwell, don't move to the notation wall, don't leave yours. Then, from above, the sound comes again: not a crash this time, but a slow, scraping drag — something being moved across stone on the third terrace, or someone who has fallen trying to move themselves. It carries down the stairwell with the chalk smell and the cold.

Kon's ear swivels first. Then its whole body turns on the step — not alarmed, not fleeing, but *oriented*, the way a compass needle finds north. It glances back at you once, a single beat of attention, and then looks up into the dark above.

turns.player: I'll go to waymarker for them, I'll ask Ossian how long he thinks everyone here will survive to give me some idea for a timeline.
turns.summary: Ossian gives survival estimates: two days for the fallen Cogitant, eight for the rest — seven to be safe. Waymarker is the mission.
turns.narration:
You revise the offer before Ossian can answer the first one.

"I'll go to Waymarker," you tell him. No qualification, no hedge — the same voice you'd use telling someone which net to mend first. "You need someone who can talk to the settlements and move fast. That's me. But I need to know how long everyone here has. Give me a number."

Ossian's eyes hold yours for a moment — not warmly, not coldly, with the precision of a man consulting figures he already has. When he speaks, it's in the same register he'd use to report a tributary's flow rate.

"Araveth and the upper-terrace Cogitants: eight to twelve days before irreversible cognitive degradation from caloric deficit — they will continue to function, apparently, until they do not. The woman on the floor." He does not look at her. "Two days. Possibly three, given her mass and the temperature in here. The Cogitant on the third terrace —" a pause, brief and considered, the first hesitation he's shown "— I do not have sufficient data. I have not seen them in four days. That number may already be wrong in the direction you'd prefer it not to be." His hands remain folded. "Eight days is your useful window. Seven is safer. After that, you are retrieving rather than rescuing."

Kon, still on the step above you, shifts weight from one forefoot to the other — impatient in the particular way that means it has already made up its mind about the next thing and is waiting for you to catch up. Above, the stairwell is quiet now. That quiet feels recent.

turns.player: "Then I'll take her with me, if she can only last two or three more days she surely won't last long enough for me to get to Waymarker and back, how far is it to Waymarker anyways, do you have a map?"
turns.summary: Ossian gives directions to Waymarker — eleven kilometers northeast — and confirms the fallen Cogitant's chances improve if she travels with Saehara.
turns.narration:
Ossian's gaze moves to the fallen Cogitant without any visible shift in feeling — the way a man checks a clock. "Waymarker is approximately eleven kilometers northeast," he says. "Footpaths are maintained to the junction cairn, then northeast along the dry-stone wall. Three hours at a reasonable pace. Less if you move with intent." He pauses. "There is no map. The Cogitants who made the last survey did not produce one for external use. The path is not complex."

He considers the second part of your proposal — taking her — with the same unhurried precision. "Her condition presents a transport problem," he says. "She is semiconscious, approximately fifty-one kilograms, and her thermoregulation is compromised. Movement in cold morning air, unsupported, will accelerate decline. Supported — carried, kept warm — the outcome changes. The Waymarker settlement has food. If she arrives, she can eat. The calculation is favorable." He says this as though he is discussing a variable he has been quietly weighing since you walked in, which he almost certainly has.

Kon drops off the first step and pads directly to the fallen Cogitant. The fox sits beside her — not touching, but present, the ambient warmth of its coat close enough that the air near the woman is measurably different. It looks at you over its shoulder. The meaning is not complicated.

You have what Ossian can give you: eleven kilometers, northeast, the junction cairn, the dry-stone wall, three hours. No map. The fallen Cogitant breathes slowly, lips still tracing something — some calculation that hasn't stopped even as her body has. Your Traveler's Pack is behind you, buckle undone. The waterskin is on her chest. Morning light cuts pale and cold through the tall windows.

turns.player: Before you lift her, press Ossian once more: is there anything you must say or show the Waymarker settlement to reopen the supply arrangement — a name, a phrase, a sign they'll recognize?
turns.summary: Ossian names the runner — Brenn — and the contact at Waymarker — Lenten the builder — and raises a third possibility: the evacuation proposal may have poisoned the arrangement.
turns.narration:
Ossian's hands remain folded. He processes the question the way water finds a crack — not with effort, but with the pressure of what he is.

"The runner's name was Brenn," he says. "A young man, freckled, with a pronounced scar along his left jaw. He knew the Cogitarium by its smell — chalk and cold iron — and he knew to come to the east archway. He was paid in notation." A pause so brief it is almost not one. "The notation was theoretical work — tensile load calculations, grain storage optimization, the kind of applied problem Waymarker's builder uses. We paid in thought. It was a sufficient arrangement."

He lets that land, then adds the thing that actually answers your question: "If you arrive and they have doubt, you may say the arrangement was settled by Lenten — the builder. Lenten will know the smell of the chalk-work and will know the runner went quiet. We do not know why Brenn stopped coming." His eyes move to the arch, then back. "We have not known for nineteen days. It is possible something happened to him. It is possible Waymarker withdrew the arrangement without notification. The calculation has a third branch" — the faintest flattening of register — "which is that the evacuation proposal was received with more hostility than described to us."

Kon lifts its head from beside the fallen Cogitant and looks at you, unhurried. That is everything Ossian has. The hall holds the chalk-smell, the pale morning light, Araveth's finger moving without pause at the wall, the woman's lips still working their silent arithmetic. The stairwell curves upward into dark. Waymarker is northeast.

turns.player: Lift the fallen Cogitant, settle her weight against your back, and head for the arch — Waymarker, now.
turns.summary: Saehara lifts the fallen Cogitant onto his back and walks out of the Cogitarium into the morning mist — northeast toward Waymarker.
turns.narration:
You kneel beside her and work your arms under her shoulders. She weighs what Ossian said she would — fifty-one kilograms of someone who forgot to eat — and her head lolls against your neck with a faint, wet exhale, lips still moving. The chalk on her fingers transfers to your sleeve. You shift her weight until it settles across your back, arms hooked under your own, her feet barely grazing the floor. Manageable. Not comfortable, but manageable.

Kon is already at the arch. It stood the moment you knelt, and now it waits in the pale mouth of the doorway, coat catching the weak morning light, tail low and steady. Not hurrying you. Just ready.

You look back once. Ossian hasn't moved — hands folded, eyes open, already calculating something else. Araveth's finger traces the same chalk loop it has traced for eleven days. The drifting Cogitants continue their slow arcs at the hall's edges, orbiting inward, orbiting inward. You don't say goodbye. There's no one here who would register it.

Then you step through the arch into the mist, the Cogitant's weight warm and still against your back, the junction cairn somewhere ahead, the dry-stone wall beyond that, and Waymarker eleven kilometers northeast. The path is not complex. You move with intent.

turns.player: Use Total Focus to push through the weight and the cold and hold a clear head for whatever Waymarker looks like when you arrive
turns.summary: Saehara pushes northeast through the mist — the weight tells, focus won't hold, the cairn still ahead.
turns.narration:
The path is longer in the body than it was in Ossian's arithmetic.

Three hours at pace, he said. You have been walking for what feels like two, and the junction cairn still hasn't come. The fallen Cogitant's weight has settled into your shoulders and lower back the way a stone settles into mud — not sudden, but total. You try to draw your focus down to something clean and tight: the next step, the cairn, Waymarker. Total Focus is a skill you trust. But the lattice here is thin, the morning cold is pressing in at your collar, and fifty-one kilograms does not negotiate with concentration. What you find instead of clarity is the particular dull ache of a mind that has been asked to be sharp while the body is busy complaining. The calculation that felt simple in the entrance hall — three hours, food, Lenten, reopen the line — has started to blur at the edges. You catch yourself thinking about the third terrace. About Ossian's hands, folded and still. About whether the dragging sound was someone alive or something settling.

Kon stops on the path ahead. Turns back. Looks at you with those too-aware eyes, and then sits — not in warning, just in witness. It does not rush you. It has been pacing ahead at a measured distance all morning, checking back like something that knows the difference between a person walking and a person grinding themselves down. The mist is thinning but the air is still cold, and the Cogitant's head is heavy against the side of your neck, her lips moving against your jaw in that silent, ceaseless counting.

You are not broken. You are tired and cold and carrying someone who has forgotten she has a body, and your focus slipped before it could lock. The cairn will come. But it will come at the pace of a man who is working, not a man who has mastered the work.

turns.player: Trust Kon — follow wherever it moves, let it navigate while you keep your head down and your legs moving
turns.summary: Saehara follows Kon northeast along the dry-stone wall — junction cairn found, Waymarker's smoke visible ahead.
turns.narration:
The junction cairn appears when Kon stops.

You hadn't seen the fox slow — it had been moving ahead of you in that particular way it has, not quite leading and not quite following, occupying some middle distance that feels like it belongs to another grammar of travel. Then it simply sits, tail wrapped around its paws, and you look up from the ground in front of your feet and there it is: four flat stones stacked on a fifth, tilted slightly northeast as if the whole thing is pointing. Someone placed it with intention. It has been here a long time.

You set your teeth and shift the Cogitant's weight on your shoulders. Her lips are still moving — you've stopped trying to hear the words. Whatever she's calculating, she's been at it since the Cogitarium floor. Her hands have found two fistfuls of your collar and they hold with a grip that surprises you each time you think about it: the body knowing what the mind has forgotten. Kon stands, shakes itself once, and moves northeast. The dry-stone wall materializes out of the scrubland a hundred meters on — low and mossy, older than any living memory, running straight into the brightening distance. The mist is thinner here. Somewhere ahead and below there is the smell of woodsmoke, which means people, which means Waymarker is real and not merely a number Ossian gave you.

You fall in behind the fox and follow the wall. The smoke smell grows. Maybe thirty minutes at this pace. The fallen Cogitant's grip hasn't loosened, but her breathing has shallowed slightly — not alarming, not yet, but a thing to note. Kon moves with purpose, not urgency. Something reads right about what lies ahead.

turns.player: Arrive and go straight to Lenten — ask the first sensible-looking person where to find the builder
turns.summary: Saehara arrives at Waymarker and is directed to Lenten's long shed across the work-yard.
turns.narration:
The woodsmoke smell arrives before Waymarker does.

You come over a low rise and the settlement opens below you — not large, maybe forty structures clustered around a central work-yard, the kind of place that builds things and knows it. Scaffolding on two buildings at the near edge. Stacked timber. The sound of a draw-plane somewhere inside, a clean sustained scrape that means someone is already at work. Morning light catches the dust on the yard's packed earth. A dog lifts its head from a doorstep, decides you're not interesting, and puts it down again.

The first person you find is a woman in an apron carrying a bucket of iron fixings toward the scaffolding — you step into her path and ask for Lenten. She looks at you: a stranger with a fox at his heels and a limp woman across his back, arrived from the direction of nowhere in particular. Her eyes move from the Cogitant's grey robes to your face. Whatever she makes of it, she answers without fuss. Lenten's at the long shed. That one. A tilt of her head toward a low building at the yard's far edge, its wide doors open, the draw-plane sound coming from inside.

Kon has already angled that way. You follow, the Cogitant's weight familiar now in the way of things you've carried too long — the ache has gone past complaint and settled into something quieter. Her lips are still moving against your jaw. Whatever count she is running, it hasn't stopped. You cross the yard, step through the open doors into the smell of fresh wood and sawdust and the particular cool dark of a working shed, and look for Lenten.

turns.player: Set the Cogitant down first — find a bench, get her off your back before you say a word
turns.summary: Saehara lowers the fallen Cogitant onto a workbench and takes stock of the shed.
turns.narration:
The first bench you find is the nearest one — long, heavy, sawdust-thick along its edge. You turn, get your knees against it, and lower the Cogitant by degrees: one hand at her back, the other catching her legs as the weight transfers. She settles onto the wood with that terrible neatness she seems to carry even unconscious, robes pooling around her, chalk-grey hands folded in her lap as if she arranged them herself. Her lips are still moving.

You straighten and feel your back make its opinions known — a deep ache from the base of your spine up through both shoulders, the kind that will be louder tomorrow. You roll your neck once, hear it crack, and breathe.

Kon has already moved deeper into the shed, nose low, the way it navigates unfamiliar spaces — methodical, unhurried, reading the place on its own terms. The draw-plane sound has stopped. Somewhere in the dimmer interior, past the stacked timber and the tool-hung wall, there is probably a person who noticed you come in and is deciding whether to say so.

The Cogitant's chest rises and falls. Small, steady. You have time to find Lenten before that changes — not much, but some.

turns.player: Call into the shed — announce yourself plainly and ask for Lenten by name
turns.summary: Saehara calls for Lenten in the shed and is answered — the builder surfaces from the dim and takes in the Cogitant on the bench.
turns.narration:
The shed smells of fresh shavings and linseed oil. You turn toward the deeper dark past the stacked timber and raise your voice to a carrying level — not a shout, just the flat projection of someone who's walked far enough to be done with being quiet about it.

"Lenten. I'm looking for Lenten. My name is Saehara Makashi — I've come from the Cogitarium."

The draw-plane sound, which had already stopped, stays stopped. Kon is somewhere ahead of you in the dimmer part of the shed, nose down, moving in slow arcs. Then a shape resolves: a broad person in a leather apron sets down a half-planed board and turns. They are somewhere past fifty, hair cut short and practical, hands so habituated to the work they are still curved as if holding a tool even empty. They take in Saehara. They take in the Cogitant on the bench — the grey robes, the chalk hands, the lips still moving — and their face does something complicated and then goes still.

"I'm Lenten." The voice is even, the way a person's voice goes even when they are deciding something quickly. Their eyes move from the woman on the bench back to you, and they make the calculation — carrier, arrival, condition — in roughly two seconds. "How long has she been like this?"

turns.player: Answer directly — give Lenten the facts as Ossian laid them out: two to three days when you left, three hours of travel since, she needs food and water now.
turns.summary: Saehara delivers Ossian's assessment; Lenten moves immediately to food and water for the Cogitant, then asks for the full picture.
turns.narration:
Lenten's eyes move from you to the Cogitant on the bench and back. You give them the facts the way Ossian gave them to you — clean and in order.

"She was at two, maybe three days when I left the Cogitarium. That was this morning — three hours of walking. She swallowed water on the road but nothing solid. She needs food now. Water too, more of it." You pause, then add: "Ossian said her mass and the cold were the main factors. She's warmer now than she was."

Lenten doesn't interrupt. Their gaze tracks to the Cogitant's chalk hands, her moving lips, the slow shallow rise and fall of her chest — the rapid, precise assessment of someone who makes calculations with their eyes before they open their mouth. A beat of quiet. Kon has stopped its slow arcs somewhere near the far timber stack and is watching Lenten the way it watches things it hasn't decided about yet.

"Right," Lenten says. The word lands like a decision already made. They move to a rough shelf at the shed's back wall — a tin cup, a covered clay pot, a heel of hard bread wrapped in cloth. They bring it all to the bench, pour water into the cup, and press it gently to the Cogitant's lips. Her throat moves. The animal reflex is still there, still working. Lenten tears the bread into small pieces without comment, sets them within reach, and straightens.

They look at you then — really look, the way they looked at the Cogitant, measuring. "You walked her eleven kilometers on your back." It isn't a question. "The Cogitarium sent one person." A pause, shorter this time. "Tell me the rest of it."

turns.player: Give Lenten the full situation — the nineteen missing days, the remaining Cogitants' timelines, the broken supply line, Brenn's absence
turns.summary: Saehara gives Lenten the full account; Lenten reveals Brenn was injured on the wall-path and the gap was a miscommunication — and asks about the third terrace.
turns.narration:
You pull a crate over and sit on it — not for comfort, just to stop performing readiness when your legs have nothing left to prove. Lenten stands at the bench, one hand resting near the clay pot, listening.

You lay it out in full. Nineteen days since the last delivery. Brenn stopped coming after the Cogitarium's evacuation proposal was brought to three settlements and dismissed — you don't know if those events are connected, but the timing holds. The Cogitants don't send for things; they wait, and in this case they kept waiting until the woman now on Lenten's bench was the third to fall. Ossian has eight to twelve days — same for the upper-terrace Cogitants — but Ossian's numbers for themselves were delivered in the same flat tone as all the others, so you suspect they've already discounted their own survival as a variable. There's also a Cogitant on the third terrace unseen for four days. You were planning to go up before the woman in front of you made the math unavoidable.

Lenten is quiet for a moment after you finish. Their thumb moves once along the edge of the workbench — the same small motion you'd make if you were running a calculation through your fingers instead of your head. Then: "Brenn came back hurt. Two weeks back, maybe a little more. Took a bad step on the wall-path — the dry-stone section about a kilometer out. Ankle. He's been off it since." They look at you steadily. "He didn't tell me the run had stopped. I thought he'd gone back out." A beat. "I didn't check."

Kon has settled into a curl near the timber stack — not quite at ease, but not watching the door either. The Cogitant swallows a second mouthful of bread on reflex, lips still moving around it. Lenten straightens and looks at the shed's wide open doors, where afternoon light is beginning its slow lean toward gold. "The supply line can be reopened. That's not the problem — I can have someone on the path with a pack before evening. But you said the third terrace." They say it the way you'd note a load-bearing issue in a structure. "What's up there?"

turns.player: Ask Lenten to get the supply runner moving tonight, then tell them you need to go back to the Cogitarium — the third terrace won't wait for you to rest
turns.summary: Saehara commits to returning to the Cogitarium tonight; Lenten agrees to dispatch a runner before evening.
turns.narration:
You lean forward on the crate, elbows on knees. "The runner — can they go before evening? Tonight if possible." You keep your voice level, not urgent, because urgency would let Lenten think there's room to negotiate the timeline. "The Cogitants at the top of the building have eight to twelve days. That's Ossian's number, and Ossian doesn't pad. Regular deliveries starting again is the thing that matters most right now."

Lenten nods once — the compact, decisive kind that means it's already done in their head. They move to the shed's far corner and pull a length of rope off a peg, setting it on the half-planed board as if it needs to be somewhere while they think. "I'll have someone on the path before the light turns. They know the route." A pause. "I'll make sure they know about the dry-stone section."

You stand. Your legs register the eleven kilometers without enthusiasm, but they hold. "Good. I'm going back." You pick up your Traveler's Pack and settle the straps — the weight is lighter now without the Cogitant across your shoulders, though your back hasn't quite registered that yet. "There's a Cogitant on the third terrace who hasn't been seen in four days. Something fell. I heard it moving when I left." You glance at the woman on the bench — still chewing on reflex, lips still tracing silent notation, chalk hands folded in her lap like a completed proof. "She's in good hands here."

Lenten watches you from across the bench. There's something in the look — not quite gratitude, not quite calculation, somewhere between the two. "You carried her eleven kilometers and you're going back tonight." It isn't a question. Kon uncurls from near the timber stack, shakes once, and moves to the open shed doors — already oriented, already done with Waymarker. The afternoon light outside is going gold at the edges. Three hours back means arriving in the early dark.

turns.player: Take a few minutes to eat and drink before the return march — the road back will demand it
turns.summary: Saehara eats and drinks before the return march — Lenten feeds the Cogitant; Kon waits at the door.
turns.narration:
The clay pot still has bread in it — Lenten doesn't object when you pull a piece off and sit back down on the crate. You eat without ceremony, the same way you walked: practical, no performance. The bread is dense and a little dry, the kind baked to last, and it does what it needs to do. You wash it down from your waterskin, two long pulls, and feel the hollow in your chest fill slightly. Not enough. Enough for the road.

Kon hasn't moved from the doorway. The fox sits framed in the afternoon light, coat the color of banked coals, already facing northeast. Waiting with the particular patience of something that has decided and is simply letting you catch up.

Lenten watches you eat without comment. They've coiled the rope and set it aside, and their hands are curved at their sides the way hands get when the day's work is already planned and running in the head. The fallen Cogitant on the bench swallows another small piece of bread on reflex — Lenten's doing, patient and quiet, not looking at her while they do it. The chalk hands are still in her lap. Her lips still move. Her breathing is steady.

"Wall-path section," Lenten says, not looking up from the Cogitant. "About a kilometer out from the Cogitarium. The dry-stone drops off sharp on the right — your runner will know, but remind whoever goes tonight anyway." They glance at you then. "Road back is the same road you came in on. You know what it costs."

turns.player: Stand, settle the pack, and go — no more words needed
turns.summary: Saehara stands, exchanges a final nod with Lenten, and walks out of the long shed into the afternoon yard — Kon already leading northeast.
turns.narration:
You stand. The crate scrapes back an inch on the shed floor and that's the whole announcement of your departure — no ceremony, no drawn-out farewell. You settle the Traveler's Pack across both shoulders, check the waterskin by feel, and roll your neck once against the stiffness that eleven kilometers of carrying has left behind.

Lenten looks up from the bench. The fallen Cogitant sits beside them, chalk hands folded in her lap, lips still moving through whatever calculation holds her somewhere else entirely. Lenten meets your eyes and gives the same compact nod they gave when they agreed to send the runner — a thing already done, acknowledged, closed. "Wall-path drop," they say, a single quiet reminder. You dip your chin. You remember.

Kon is off the doorframe before you reach it, dropping silently from seated to moving with no visible transition in between. The fox pads out into the afternoon yard ahead of you — coat warm-ember in the long gold light, already angled northeast, already home in the road the way you've never quite been anywhere. The central work-yard opens around you: stacked timber, the sound of a draw-plane from somewhere further in, two workers who glance at the grey robes through the shed's open door and then look back at their work. You pass through without stopping.

The Cogitarium is eleven kilometers behind the horizon. The third terrace hasn't waited four days to start waiting for you now.

turns.player: Watch Kon as you walk — the fox knows when the world goes thin; let it tell you if the road north carries anything worth knowing before you arrive
turns.summary: Saehara watches Kon on the return march — the fox reads the road as clear; the dry-stone wall hazard is navigated without incident.
turns.narration:
The road northeast unrolls under your boots in long gold light — the afternoon tilting toward evening without hurry, the pale stone of the Cogitarium not yet visible through the scrub. Kon runs ahead by twenty paces, sometimes thirty, coat deep red against the bleached gravel and winter-dry grass. You watch it the way you'd watch water for a current: not staring, just keeping it in the corner of your eye.

For the first kilometer the fox is simply a fox-shaped thing moving efficiently. It checks the wind, noses at a tuft of dry sedge, diverts around a standing stone without breaking rhythm. Normal enough. Then the dry-stone wall rises on your left and Kon angles toward it, then away — and doesn't look back, which is the tell. When the fox wants you to see something it looks back. When it simply adjusts, it's reading the terrain as home.

Halfway along the wall-section you feel the path drop sharply to your right — the hazard Lenten named, a meter of hidden dip where the gravel gives way to loose scree. You step wide without breaking stride, and Kon, ahead of you, has already done the same. The fox knew. It will know the wall-path drop on the return in the dark as well, which is useful information: you won't have to slow for it, just watch where Kon steps.

The rest of the wall-path gives nothing unusual. Kon doesn't go still. The coat doesn't warm. The scrub on either side holds ordinary late-afternoon sound — a single bird, the creak of wind in dead stems — and that absence of strangeness is its own answer. Whatever the Cogitarium holds, the road north is clean. You bank that fact and keep walking, the Cogitarium's pale towers just beginning to resolve against the sky ahead.

turns.player: Call to Ossian as you enter — announce you've returned and the supply line is reopening tonight
turns.summary: Saehara returns to the Cogitarium entrance hall — Ossian surfaces to ask about the fallen Cogitant; Kon orients toward the stairwell.
turns.narration:
The arch is exactly where you left it — pale stone, chalk-dust smell, the particular cold of a building that forgets to heat itself because no one inside has remembered to be cold. You step through into the entrance hall and the sound of your boots changes register: harder, flatter, the echo of a space that is mostly emptiness and calculation.

"Ossian." You don't shout. The hall carries well enough that you don't need to. "The supply line reopens tonight. Lenten is dispatching a runner before evening."

Araveth is still at the wall. You can see that immediately — the same rigid posture, the same finger moving through the same chalk notation, the same total absence of reaction to your arrival or your voice. Eleven days at that wall and your presence registers exactly as much as the ambient temperature change of a door opening: nothing. The watershed model spreads behind them, tributaries and particulate counts and flow rates marching in tight columns across the stone.

Ossian is cross-legged where you left them. The same spot. The same folded hands. They surface from whatever internal calculation occupied the hours of your absence the way a tide goes slack — not startled, simply returning from somewhere else. Their eyes find you, and there is the same dense quality to their attention: a mind that does not skim.

"The woman?" they ask. First question. Not the supply line — the timeline.

Kon slips past your ankle and into the hall, coat settling from the road's cold, already oriented toward the stairwell in the far corner. The fox doesn't stop.

turns.player: Report and move immediately — ask Ossian to talk while you walk to the stairs
turns.summary: Saehara reports the fallen Cogitant's status to Ossian and leads the climb toward the third terrace.
turns.narration:
You don't stop walking. "She's alive," you tell Ossian. "Lenten has her — water, bread, shelter. The two-day window is still running from this morning. Lenten will send for someone qualified to bring her in if her condition changes." You're already moving toward the far corner, the stairwell's shadow growing in your peripheral vision. "The supply line reopens tonight. That's resolved."

Ossian unfolds from the floor in a single motion — not standing so much as the act of calculation relocating itself upright. They follow without comment, bare feet on chalk-dusted stone, pace matching yours with the particular ease of a body that has never learned to hurry and never needed to. "The window's margin is acceptable," they say, and the way they say it is the closest thing to relief you've heard from anyone in this building. "Lenten's competence accounts for at least forty percent of the variance."

Kon is already at the stairwell's base, one forepaw on the first step, coat reading the darkness above with the patience of something that has been still and warm before. It doesn't look back. The dragging sound — slow, cyclical, coming from somewhere two or three terraces up — arrives again like a thought you can't locate the source of. Not distress. Not violence. Something working through a problem with whatever motion remains to it.

"Third terrace," you say, more to yourself than Ossian. You put your hand to the wall at the stair's edge — stone cold through your palm — and look up into the narrowing dark. "Four days. What are we going to find?"

quests.title: Reopen the Supply Line
quests.summary: The Cogitarium has gone nineteen days without resupply — the runner from Waymarker stopped coming after the evacuation proposal was dismissed. Someone must go to Waymarker and reopen the arrangement before the Cogitants starve.
progress: Arrived at Waymarker — directed to Lenten's long shed at the yard's far edge. Lenten not yet found.
Cogitant safely off Saehara's back and on a workbench. Lenten not yet found — somewhere deeper in the shed.
Cogitant delivered to Waymarker and receiving care. Lenten is present and engaged — now asking for the full situation before any formal arrangement can be discussed.

At his hip a long katana with a light-blue-wrapped handle and a tanto with a white-wrapped handle, both blades showing Damascus wave-pattern steel. A worn satchel carries loose pages and a quill.

bio.motivation: He had a vision of writing his own life in a book and teaching generations to come with the knowledge he drafts into the book.
bio.story: Saehara has honed his craft in blades and travelling. He left his village of Chisai seeking adventure and a journey to draft into his book he is writing. He is hoping something will help him come up with the title for this book.

lenten.name: Lenten
lenten.role: Builder at Waymarker — the one who made the Cogitarium's supply line work, and who knows exactly what it cost to make it work
lenten.voiceHints: Speaks in load-bearing sentences — nothing decorative, nothing wasted, but not cold. Measures what he says the way he measures timber: cut once, cut right. Has a builder's habit of naming the thing underneath the thing — the joist behind the wall, the assumption behind the proposal. Goes quiet when he's calculating, and the quiet feels occupied, not absent. Early-spring mud still on his boots.
knowledge: The Cogitarium supply line in full — every dependency, every seasonal choke point, every person who makes it hold
Which parts of the evacuation proposal would snap the line immediately, and which would fray it slow
What Waymarker's builders actually believe, as opposed to what they've been willing to say out loud
That abstract plans have a weight and it falls on specific people — he has watched it fall
lenten.wants: For the decision — whatever it turns out to be — to be made by people who have walked the route and felt the cold, not only by people who have thought about it well.
lenten.fears: That the evacuation debate will be resolved at the level of argument before anyone has checked what argument costs on the ground, and that he will have been too careful to say so clearly enough.

establishedFacts.text: A Cogitant collapsed in the entrance hall — the third this week. Ossian noted it without alarm. The Cogitarium's bodies are failing at an accelerating rate.
Saehara has taken the fallen Cogitant from the Cogitarium and is carrying her northeast toward Waymarker — she cannot be left behind; her two-to-three day window is now the mission's pace.
Lenten has committed to dispatching a supply runner to the Cogitarium before evening — the supply line is formally reopening tonight.
